questions.question: What does 'git rebase' do to your branch's commits?
options: Deletes them permanently from the repository
Replays them on top of another branch, creating a linear history
Copies them into a stash for later use
Squashes all commits into a single merge commit
questions.answer: Replays them on top of another branch, creating a linear history

questions.question: After rebasing feature-x onto main, what happens to the original commit hashes on feature-x?
options: They stay exactly the same
Only the first commit gets a new hash
They change because the commits now have different parent commits
They are removed and replaced with a single hash
questions.answer: They change because the commits now have different parent commits

questions.question: What is the Golden Rule of rebasing?
options: Always rebase before every commit
Never rebase commits that have been pushed to a shared remote
Only rebase on the main branch
Never rebase more than three commits at a time
questions.answer: Never rebase commits that have been pushed to a shared remote

questions.question: In a merge workflow, what is created to join two diverging branches?
options: A rebase commit
A squash commit
A merge commit
A cherry-pick commit
questions.answer: A merge commit

questions.question: After rebasing a feature branch onto main, what type of merge can be performed?
options: A three-way merge
A recursive merge
A fast-forward merge
An octopus merge
questions.answer: A fast-forward merge

questions.question: Why is a fast-forward merge possible after rebasing?
options: Because rebase deletes all previous commits
Because the feature branch is now directly ahead of main with no divergence
Because Git automatically resolves all conflicts during rebase
Because fast-forward merges work regardless of branch structure
questions.answer: Because the feature branch is now directly ahead of main with no divergence

questions.question: Which command do you run to rebase the current branch onto main?
options: git merge main --rebase
git rebase main
git rebase --onto main
git pull --rebase main
questions.answer: git rebase main

questions.question: When a rebase encounters a conflict, what does Git do?
options: Automatically resolves the conflict using the incoming changes
Deletes the conflicting file
Pauses the rebase and asks you to resolve the conflict
Aborts the rebase entirely and reverts all changes
questions.answer: Pauses the rebase and asks you to resolve the conflict

questions.question: After resolving a conflict during a rebase, what two commands should you run in order?
options: git commit and git rebase --continue
git add <file> and git rebase --continue
git rebase --continue and git add <file>
git stash and git rebase --continue
questions.answer: git add <file> and git rebase --continue

questions.question: Which command aborts a rebase that is in progress?
options: git rebase --cancel
git rebase --abort
git rebase --stop
git rebase --undo
questions.answer: git rebase --abort

questions.question: What happens to the branch when you run 'git rebase --abort'?
options: All commits on the branch are deleted
The branch is merged into main automatically
The branch returns to its pre-rebase state with nothing lost
The branch is detached from HEAD
questions.answer: The branch returns to its pre-rebase state with nothing lost

questions.question: When is it safe to use rebase?
options: Only on the main branch after a release
On local branches that haven't been pushed, or personal branches only you use
Anytime, regardless of whether others have the commits
Only when there are no conflicts expected
questions.answer: On local branches that haven't been pushed, or personal branches only you use

questions.question: In the rebase diagram, what do C' and D' represent compared to C and D?
options: Exact copies with the same hashes
Commits with the same content but different hashes due to different parent commits
Commits that were squashed together
Commits that were cherry-picked from another repository
questions.answer: Commits with the same content but different hashes due to different parent commits

questions.question: Which command shows a visual graph of all branches in the log?
options: git log --all --visual
git log --oneline --graph --all
git log --branches --tree
git log --show-graph --all-branches
questions.answer: git log --oneline --graph --all

questions.question: What is the main advantage of rebase over merge?
options: It is always faster to execute
It creates a clean, linear history without merge commits
It never causes conflicts
It preserves the full branching history
questions.answer: It creates a clean, linear history without merge commits

questions.question: What is the main advantage of merge over rebase?
options: It creates a linear history
It preserves the full branching history
It rewrites commit hashes
It is the only way to combine branches
questions.answer: It preserves the full branching history

questions.question: Why does rebasing rewrite history?
options: Because it changes the commit messages
Because it changes the author of the commits
Because the replayed commits have new parent commits, resulting in new hashes
Because it removes the original branch entirely
questions.answer: Because the replayed commits have new parent commits, resulting in new hashes

questions.question: What problem does rebasing pushed commits cause for other developers?
options: Their local repository becomes read-only
Their copies of the original commits conflict with the rewritten history
Git will automatically delete their local branches
They will lose access to the remote repository
questions.answer: Their copies of the original commits conflict with the rewritten history

questions.question: After rebasing and fast-forward merging, what command safely deletes the merged feature branch?
options: git branch -D feature-x
git branch -d feature-x
git delete feature-x
git remove branch feature-x
questions.answer: git branch -d feature-x

questions.question: Which command creates and switches to a new branch in one step?
options: git branch feature-x && git checkout feature-x
git switch -c feature-x
git new-branch feature-x
git create feature-x
questions.answer: git switch -c feature-x

questions.question: In the side-by-side comparison exercise, how do you view the last 6 commits with a graph?
options: git log --oneline --graph -6
git log --last 6 --graph
git log --oneline --limit=6
git show --graph --count 6
questions.answer: git log --oneline --graph -6

questions.question: Which command checks the current state of files during a rebase conflict?
options: git rebase --status
git status
git conflict --list
git check
questions.answer: git status

questions.question: What does the content of the commits look like after a rebase?
options: The content is completely different from the originals
The content is the same but the history is rewritten
The content is merged with the base branch content
The content is compressed into a single diff
questions.answer: The content is the same but the history is rewritten

questions.question: When you rebase feature-y onto main and there is a conflict in conflict-file.txt, what must you do with the conflict markers?
options: Leave them in the file so Git can track the conflict
Remove the markers and choose the correct content, then stage the file
Delete the entire file and recreate it
Run git resolve --auto to handle them
questions.answer: Remove the markers and choose the correct content, then stage the file

questions.question: In the merge vs. rebase comparison, what is the key visual difference in 'git log --graph' output?
options: Merge shows tags while rebase does not
Rebase shows commit authors while merge does not
Merge shows a branching and merging pattern while rebase shows a straight line
There is no visual difference between the two
questions.answer: Merge shows a branching and merging pattern while rebase shows a straight line
